abstract: This Anthropic Economic Index report analyzes survey responses from approximately 81,000 Claude users to understand public perceptions of AI's economic impacts. Using Claude-powered classifiers to infer occupation, career stage, productivity gains, and concerns from open-ended responses, the authors find that workers in occupations with higher observed AI exposure express more concern about job displacement, while reporting substantial productivity gains—particularly in the highest- and lowest-paid occupations. Roughly one-fifth of respondents voiced concern about economic displacement, with early-career workers and those reporting the largest AI-driven speedups showing the most anxiety.
key_claims: Perceived job threat from AI correlates positively with observed AI exposure at the occupational level, suggesting workers' intuitions track actual AI diffusion patterns.
Early-career workers express substantially more concern about AI-driven job displacement than senior workers.
Productivity gains from AI follow a U-shaped distribution across the wage spectrum, with both high-paid (e.g., software developers) and some low-paid workers reporting the largest benefits.
Scope expansion (doing new tasks previously out of reach) is the most commonly cited productivity benefit, followed by speed.
Respondents reporting the largest AI-driven speedups also express the highest concern about job displacement, suggesting that rapid task acceleration heightens uncertainty about role viability.
Most respondents perceive AI productivity surplus as accruing to themselves rather than to employers, clients, or AI companies, though this varies by career stage.
methods: Analysis of open-ended survey responses from 80,508 users of personal Claude.ai accounts.

Use of Claude-powered classifiers to infer respondent attributes (occupation, career stage) and sentiments (job displacement concern, productivity gain magnitude, type of productivity benefit) from free-form text.
Productivity ratings on a 1-7 scale derived from response content, with anchored examples for calibration.
Linkage of inferred occupations to the 'observed exposure' measure from Massenkoff and McCrory (2026), reflecting the share of an occupation's tasks performed by Claude.
Robustness check restricting analysis to the 11% of respondents who explicitly stated their occupation.
Cross-tabulation of perceived job threat by exposure quartile, career stage, and inferred speedup level.
findings: About 20% of respondents voiced concern about economic displacement from AI.

Every 10-percentage-point increase in observed occupational exposure was associated with a 1.3-percentage-point increase in perceived job threat; the top exposure quartile mentioned job threat three times as often as the bottom quartile.
Mean self-reported productivity rating was 5.1 ('substantially more productive'); only 3% reported negative or neutral impacts, while 42% gave no clear productivity indication.
Management occupations (largely entrepreneurs/solopreneurs) and computer/math occupations reported the highest productivity gains; scientific and legal professions reported the mildest.
48% of respondents who discussed productivity cited scope expansion; 40% cited speed gains; smaller shares cited quality and cost benefits.
60% of early-career workers said they personally benefited from AI, compared to 80% of senior professionals.
About 10% of respondents who named a beneficiary said employers or clients were extracting the gains by demanding more work.

The relationship between reported speedup and perceived job threat is U-shaped: those slowed down by AI (e.g., some creative workers) and those experiencing the largest speedups both expressed elevated displacement concerns.
contributions: Links qualitative survey evidence on worker sentiment to a quantitative behavioral measure of AI exposure derived from Claude usage data.
Demonstrates a methodology for using LLM-based classifiers to extract structured economic variables (occupation, career stage, productivity dimensions) from open-ended interview transcripts.
Provides early empirical evidence that worker anxiety about AI tracks actual occupational AI penetration.
Documents heterogeneity in AI productivity benefits across wage levels and occupational groups, including underappreciated gains for some low-wage workers using AI for side ventures.
Surfaces the U-shaped relationship between reported AI speedup and job-loss concern as a hypothesis for future structured research.
framing: The paper sits within Anthropic's Economic Index research program, which seeks to characterize AI's labor-market impacts using usage data from Claude. It positions itself as complementing task-based exposure measures with self-reported worker experience, contributing to ongoing debates about AI-driven job displacement, productivity diffusion, and the distribution of AI's economic surplus between workers, employers, and AI providers.
